summary: documentation update and query functions is out of date
description:
the documentation for $collection->query looks like (note $limit, $skip):

=method query ($query)

when in fact it should be

=method query ($query, $limit, $skip)

Likewise, the $collection->update method, specifies:

=method update ($object, $upsert?)

Should be (note $query):

 =method update ($query, $object, $upsert)

summary: rt.cpan.org#52648 - MongoDB 0.26 and Perl 5.8.7, Newx(z) with 3 args
description:
Hello and thank you for MongoDB 0.26,

I also created a rt.cpan.org ticket that contains the same information, hope you don't mind me also creating this issue, I wasn't sure which you would prefer.

  https://rt.cpan.org/Ticket/Display.html?id=52648

I'm running Perl 5.8.7 (on x86_64-linux)

Running make produces:

cc -c -I. -fno-strict-aliasing -pipe -I/usr/local/include
-D_LARGEFILE_SOURCE -D_FILE_OFFSET_BITS=64 -I/usr/include/gdbm -O2
-DVERSION=\"0.26\" -DXS_VERSION=\"0.26\" -o xs/Connection.o -fpic
"-I<removed>perl-5.8.7/lib/5.8.7/x86_64-linux/CORE" xs/Connection.c
xs/Connection.xs: In function `XS_MongoDB__Connection_connect':
xs/Connection.xs:52: error: syntax error before "mongo_link"
xs/Connection.xs:63: error: syntax error before "char"
xs/Connection.xs:68: error: syntax error before "char"
xs/Connection.xs:75: error: syntax error before "char"
xs/Connection.xs: In function `XS_MongoDB__Connection__insert':
xs/Connection.xs:118: error: syntax error before "char"
xs/Connection.xs: In function `XS_MongoDB__Connection__remove':
xs/Connection.xs:147: error: syntax error before "char"
xs/Connection.xs: In function `XS_MongoDB__Connection__update':
xs/Connection.xs:170: error: syntax error before "char"
make: *** [xs/Connection.o] Error 1

Line 52 of xs/Connection.xs is:

Newx(link, 1, mongo_link);

I'm using version 2.21 of ExtUtils::ParseXS

Line 122 of xs/Connection.c is:

Newx(link, 1, mongo_link);

According to http://perldoc.perl.org/perlapi.html In 5.9.3, Newx() and
friends replace the older New() API, and drops the first parameter, x, a
debug aid which allowed callers to identify themselves. Looking at
perlapi for 5.8.7
(http://search.cpan.org/~nwclark/perl-5.8.7/pod/perlapi.pod) I see:

New
The XSUB-writer's interface to the C malloc function.
void New(int id, void* ptr, int nitems, type)

No mention of Newx, so I changed line 52 of xs/Connection.xs to:

New(0, link, 1, mongo_link);

and this seemed to work, I also made the following changes:

MongoDB-0.26/mongo_link.c
245c245
> New(0, cursor->buf.start, cursor->header.length, char);
---
< Newx(cursor->buf.start, cursor->header.length, char);

MongoDB-0.26/mongo_link.h
88c88
> New(0, buf.start, size, char); \
---
< Newx(buf.start, size, char); \

MongoDB-0.26/xs/Connection.xs
52c52
> New(0, link, 1, mongo_link);
---
< Newx(link, 1, mongo_link);
63c63
> Newz(0, link->server.pair.left_host, llen+1, char);
---
< Newxz(link->server.pair.left_host, llen+1, char);
68c68
> Newz(0, link->server.pair.right_host, rlen+1, char);
---
< Newxz(link->server.pair.right_host, rlen+1, char);
75c75
> Newz(0, link->server.single.host, len+1, char);
---
< Newxz(link->server.single.host, len+1, char);

MongoDB-0.26/xs/Cursor.xs
115,116c115
> New(0, buf.start, size, char);
---
< Newx(buf.start, size, char);
190c189
> New(0, cursor, 1, mongo_cursor);
---
< Newx(cursor, 1, mongo_cursor);

make now completes without error and make test when running against the
stable version of mongod just fails 4 tests in t/collection.t (I have
not looked into these failures yet)

not ok 74
# Failed test at t/collection.t line 256.
# got: '1'
# expected: '2'
ok 75

# got: undef
# expected: '4'
not ok 77

# got: '1'
# expected: '2'
ok 78
not ok 79
# Failed test at t/collection.t line 274.
# got: undef
# expected: '4'

I've not tested the change with any other version of perl.

Hope this is of some use.

Cheers,
Peter (Stig) Edwards

summary: Modification of non-creatable hash value attempted
description:
An error can occur when dumping or serializing hashes returned from methods in this module when a hash value is undef.

For example:

  Data::Dump::dump $database->last_error();

will generate the following error:

Modification of non-creatable hash value attempted, subscript "err" at /usr/local/share/perl/5.10.0/Data/Dump.pm line 272.

if the return value is:

{
  'ok' => '1',
  'n' => 1,
  'err' => undef,
  'updatedExisting' => 1
}

This is a fairly common bug encountered when using dumpers or serializers (like YAML etc)...

Now I'm no XS expert but I've heard that newSV(0) can be used in place of &PL_sv_undef to alleviate the problem (see http://search.cpan.org/~dapm/perl-5.10.1/pod/perlguts.pod#AVs,_HVs_and_undefined_values).

There are two places in the code that need to be changed, and it seems to work... but hopefully someone with expert XS knowledge can verify that it really is that easy to fix (I'm reluctant to make a patch without such an opinion).

perl_mongo.c line 334
xs/Cursor.xs line 236

summary: Development releases of mongo-perl-driver should use developer version strings (like 0.27_01)
description:
also, I noticed that the latest update to mongo-perl-driver didn't bump the version string

<centosian2> "Versions which are still being implemented usually have the current version number with an underscore and a number, like 0.04_01 and are called "developer releases"; the underscore makes it clear that the distribution is not yet ready for the general public. You should follow that practice. You can even upload a developer's release to the CPAN. When people look at your module they will see that version, with a red sign stating it's a developer's rel
<centosian2> ease and a link to the last ready-to-take version. 
<centosian2> from http://www.perlmonks.org/?node_id=431702

summary: boolean::true and boolean::false are accepted to put boolean values in, but they are not returned to get boolean values back out
description:
(I posted this on the CPAN bugtracker before I realized the Perl Driver had its own section in JIRA, so my apologies.  The original can be found here: https://rt.cpan.org/Public/Bug/Display.html?id=60161 )

To place boolean values "true" and "false" into MongoDB using insert,  save, etc., boolean::true and boolean::false can be used.

However, when these boolean values are read back out, they are translated into 1 and 0, which are functionally equivalent, but somewhat defeat the purpose of using boolean::true and boolean::false to save the values in the first place.

To solve this, I have provided the following patch, which will attempt to call boolean::true or boolean::false appropriately for BSON_BOOL items returned from the database (and if those fail, hopefully fall back on the current behavior).

This is my first time writing C to call a Perl function, so comments or suggestions are very much welcome.  All I can say is that this is working great for me.

summary: cursor not found croak() bubbling up after upgrade?
description:
I had been running a MySQL migration tool that I wrote using the 0.33 driver against various releases of MongoDB 1.6.x using auto-sharding and 2 shards backed my 3 machine replica sets.  Today I upgraded to 1.6.2 and decided to upgrade to the latest (0.36 Perl driver).  I did a full wipe of all my data, installed 1.6.2, and tried to run my script.

It now fails on the first record with "cursor not found" being caught by my die handler.

I see this commit:

http://github.com/mongodb/mongo-perl-driver/commit/4305664d2ae1b5c67cae0beff256d1173b6ac95b

But I'm not sure if this is a long-standing bug in my code that this upgrade revealed (despite it having inserted millions of records in testing) or if something else weird is going on.  The replica sets are healthy, as are the mongos processes and config servers.  In fact, the first record *does* get inserted before it blows up.

summary: gridfs throws exception if prefix is not "fs"
description:
The Perl code for gridfs insertion runs the "filemd5" command against a root of "fs", even if a different prefix is chosen.
If there isn't an "fs.chunks" collection in the database, this causes an exception:

Assertion: 13284:best guess plan requested, but scan and order required: query: { files_id: ObjectId('...') } order: { files_id: 1, n: 1 } choices: { $natural: 1 } 

This is complaining about the lack of an index which is correct because it's been given the wrong collection prefix.

Didn't notice the problem until I upgraded the server from 1.4.x to 1.6.x, but it looks from the change history like it's been hardcoded to "fs" since the beginning.

I've developed and attached a patch against 0.37.  In order to do it correctly, I've added the prefix as an attribute of the GridFS object and pass it down on creation.  I've also taken the opportunity to refactor the code so that the chunks and files collections are built by the GridFS object itself rather than inside the Database object as that seems more sensible to me - it keeps the code in one place.  I've also added a filename index to the files collection as this mimics the behaviour of the "mongofiles" client and was missing from the Perl code originally.

I hope my changes are acceptable;  I admit they go a little bit beyond just fixing the bug, but not my much and hopefully they make sense.

Cheers,
Olly

PS> apologies for attaching the diff; don't have a github account right now; if you'd rather receive a merge request, I'll set one up.

description:

As reported by user

I've got a problem with the last version of the Perl driver. 
I used to create indexes with embedded keys, like this: 
$coll->ensure_index( { "address.state" => 1 } ); 
Since the following commit I cannot use the dot notation anymore. It 
fails with "inserts cannot contain the . character". 
http://github.com/mongodb/mongo-perl-driver/commit/0baf83a0e0b5df255d... 
Shouldn't the "." char be allowed for the inserts in the index collection ? 

Per the documentation, the dot notation should be valid

http://www.mongodb.org/display/DOCS/Indexes#Indexes-EmbeddedKeys


Resolution:
Enable users to create indexes on embedded documents

Business Case:
Backward compatibility

summary: Floating timezone handling may be surprising when converting from MySQL
description:
When inserting DateTime objects, the time is correctly adjusted to UTC and converted to epoch. However, if the floating timezone specified, the time is assumed to be UTC and no adjustments are made. 

This is almost certainly not what you want, particularly when writing scripts to migrate from MySQL -- DateTime::Format::MySQL returns floating timezone objects. 

When deflating, I would either assume that floating timezone means local, or emit a warning, since there's really no situation in which you'd use DateTime::TimeZone::Floating to mean UTC.

summary: authenticated replicas don't work when you connect to a non-primary first or you haven't crafted your url correctly
description:
Here's a patch for another bug that manifests when you have replicas and use authentication.
I'm actually fixing two problems at the same time which I know is naughty but their symptoms are related.

First issue: if you don't specify a port in your host URL, it defaults to 27017 - as it should - but doesn't include that in the host attribute.  So when it comes to refresh the host server list as part of the master detection, it doesn't re-use the connection (because "host" != "host:27017") and creates a new one.  This on its own doesn't cause problems, but does create two connections when only one will do.

Second issue: when refreshing the host list as part of master determination, the code creates individual host connections without propagating the authentication info so these do not work.  Will only cause a problem in certain situations, but is not right.

summary: connect code fails to always correctly interpret the returned perl structure from 'authenticate'
description:
Whilst developing a fix for PERL-149, I discovered another problem.  In some cases, the perl SV returned from the 'authenticate' matches the test "SvTYPE(result) != SVt_RV" even though it does contain what we want (a pointer to a hash).  I believe this is because the test is naive, especially given the variation between Perl versions for SvTYPE (I'm using 5.12 BTW, but the types were revamped in 5.10 and 5.14 so there are some subtle differences across all the 'active' releases today).  The current test will pass for an IV object, but not for a PVIV object, which is perfectly valid.

Attached is a patch which does it in a better way, IMHO.  Instead of trying to work out the type of the object, it just checks for POK (valid string = failure message) or ROK (valid reference = hash we assume).

For good measure, I've also added in some previously missing variable de-references.  The patch is against git HEAD (effectively 0.45).

summary: utf8 checking in perl_mongo.c is too lax
description:
The function isUTF8 in perl_mongo.c is too lax when it comes to UTF-8 checking. It allows f.i. a byte sequence 0xc0 0xa0 to be inserted, while the UTF-8 standard defines that multibyte sequences cannot start with 0xc0 or 0xc1.  

I'm attaching a modified function with a few tests that (I hope!) accounts for all the specifications in Unicode 6.1, Chapter 3, Table 3.7 (pg 95 of http://www.unicode.org/versions/Unicode6.1.0/ch03.pdf). Apologies for the somewhat clunky c though, especially the conversion from the char array to an int array, which works for me on a run of the mill Ubuntu 10.04, but hasn't been tested anywhere else. I'm hoping it'll be a no-op should char be unsigned...

Cheers.

summary: Improving GridFS insert and delete speed
description:
https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-perl-driver/pull/39

I noticed that for every insert and ever delete, we reinsert into system.indexes to create the two required GridFS indexes. I know have it where the indexes are created when the GridFS object is created. I also have added extra code so that if the database/collection/indexes are dropped after the GridFS object is created, it'll recreate them for the insert.

I also saw that the GridFS specification (http://www.mongodb.org/display/DOCS/GridFS+Specification) requires the files_id/n index to have the unique option. So I added that in as well.

I also removed the line in the remove() function where it created the indexes because I didn't see why it was need. I tested out deleteing from GridFS with out the indexes and It worked just fine.

description:
The use of AUTOLOAD for collection names (and any other purpose I may not have noticed) is an interface bug.  Firstly it favors collection names that don't conflict with existing Colleciton methods.   Secondly it is fragile in the face of any new methods added to the Database class.  Thirdly it hides the accidental call of methods on the wrong object; if you accidentally call $database->get_dbase, well, you don't get an error, you get ->get_database('get_dbase').

Please remove this misfeature.

summary: Perl driver speed
description:
Perl driver is very, very, very slow compared to PHP one...

Doing some tests with perl 5.10.1 and perl 5.12.3 show that PHP is between 2.8 to more than 3.2 times faster for basic insertions (without or with threads enabled).

In PHP case, mongod reach 100% and PHP stay around 57% CPU. 10M insertions done in ~100 seconds.

In Perl case, mongodb stay around 76% while Perl  is at 100% CPU ! 10M insertions done in ~280 seconds (threaded perl) or ~320 seconds (original, debian threaded perl).

I join the 2 sample scripts and a little patch that avoid calling sprintf() 12 times for each insertion. In the test case, 10% of exec time was spent in sprintf. With it we gain 10% of total time, not much, but better than nothing... :)

please update description of 'fsync' write concern flag
description:
Since your driver's documentation (e.g. [here|http://api.mongodb.org/perl/current/MongoDB/Database.html]) describes the 'fsync' write concern flag, can you please update its description so that it accurately describes the current behavior, which is:

# 'fsync' is identical to 'j' if mongod is running with a journal (today, 99% of users should be here)
# 'fsync' requests that the write be sync()'d to disk only if mongod is not running with a journal
# 'fsync' cannot be used simultaneously with the 'j' flag

The point is that:
- 'j' means 'sync via the journal if present, or error if not, or if 'fsync' is also set'
- 'fsync' means 'sync via the journal if present, or sync via msync() if there is no journal, and error if 'j' is also set'

Additionally, could you please mark the 'fsync' flag as "Deprecated; do not use", following this commit [here|https://github.com/mongodb/docs/commit/75c648730b54a920cf85372fd27ab8600b67b794]

Please see SERVER-11399 for background.

Thanks!

summary: MongoDB Client Memory Leak when Inserting records - sample code included
description:
If you insert records in a loop, the process ram usage grows forever. There seems be a memory leak in the MongoDB PERL Lib. Attached is the simple version of trivial perl script which will eat all the memory on the local machine.

If you add "Devel::Leak" you can see that MogoDB is allocating new memory in every call to collection->save and not releasing it.

This makes MongoDB very dangerous to use with long-running perl process.

CPAN tells me I am using version 0.702.2 of MongoDB. BUT That version is not available in the version list above, so I selected  0.702.1.

This is perl 5, version 14, subversion 2 (v5.14.2) built for x86_64-linux-gnu-thread-multi.

summary: Collection find_and_modify may use error string as document (croaks when using strict)
description:
Certain error conditions - I found it when testing a duplicate value in a unique-indexed field - will cause MongoDB::Collection's find_and_modify to behave incorrectly, and croak if strict is being used.

The issue begins on line 236, where the result of db->run_command is treated as a document (i.e. a hash ref).  But in the error case, this return value is a string instead.

The result should be examined more carefully before trying to use as a hash ref, or run_command should be prohibited from returning a raw string.

summary: Add ipv6 support
Ver. 0.45 is NOT thread safe
description:
The short version of this description is that the 0.45 driver is NOT thread safe despite it being claimed in the documentation.

We have used the MongoDB PERL driver for about 2 years in a setup which basically receives JSON documents using Apache/mod_perl and saves them on a single MongoDB instance. We handle many thousands of documents per minute.
We have observed a lot of leaked scalars and segmentation faults during operation, which we could sort of live with hoping the driver would get better with time (reported in https://jira.mongodb.org/browse/PERL-122).

Yesterday we expanded our setup to having two different MongoDB hosts to cope with more clients.
We immediately observed that a few documents randomly landed on the wrong host (we make separate connections for each save). 
The connections are getting MIXED in the threads!

Today we tried going for just one thread per process in Apache and that solved both the leaking and the connection mixing.
We can only conclude that the driver in NOT thread safe.

I'll be happy to supply more information about our setup and code if needed.

summary: Leak of connections on some conditions
possible error in xs/OID.xs
description:
(via email from michael.chalfant@gmail.com)

I was evaluating MongoDB-0.45 for possible use.
In the review, I noticed a logical error in xs/OID.xs

This code would be invoked if the argument to _build_value: oid_sv is not NULL.

Line 33:
Copy(oid, SvPVX(oid_sv), 24, char);

The 1st two arguments to the macro are transposed. The source is the 1st argument and the destination is the 2nd.
Since oid was predeclared but not explicitly initialized there will be unpredictable content returned from _build_value.

I'm not certain this branch is triggered during normal operation of MongoDB.

Also, perhaps related, in t/bson.t line 65 uses an undocumented form of the MongoDB::OID constructor:

"_id" => MongoDB::OID->new("49b6d9fb17330414a0c63101")

When I first read this code, I was expecting the above to create a MongoDB::OID object with the value given in the argument.
In practice, this acts as if there were no arguments to the constructor, and a new id is generated via _build_value and assigned to the object.
This allows the test script to pass.

Again, I doubt these are particularly relevant to the overall function and use of the driver, but thought you would want to be informed.
In any event, congratulations on a well-built and well-documented distribution.

summary: t/database.t fails on driver version 0.707.0.0 when tests run against 2.8.0 mongod
description:
Hey guys,

I noticed the Perl-driver test t/database.t fails on version 0.707.0.0 when a 2.8.x Mongod is running during 'make test'.

It seems the output for a non-existant db.runCommand changed in 2.8, see below:

=== Mongo 2.6.x: ===
"timtest1:PRIMARY> db.version()
2.6.4
timtest1:PRIMARY> db.runCommand({ "foo" : "bar"})
{
        "ok" : 0,
        "errmsg" : "no such cmd: foo",
        "code" : 59,
        "bad cmd" : {
                "foo" : "bar"
        }
}"

=== Mongo 2.8.x ===
"> db.version()
2.8.0-rc1
> db.runCommand({ foo : "bar"})
{
        "ok" : 0,
        "errmsg" : "no such command: foo",
        "code" : 59,
        "bad cmd" : {
                "foo" : "bar"
        }
}"

The t/database.t test fails on line 72 with the following:

"#   Failed test 'error from non-existent command'
#   at t/database.t line 72.
#                   'no such command: foo'
#     doesn't match '(?-xism:no such cmd|unrecognized command)'
# Looks like you failed 1 test of 15.
t/database.t ........... "

The reason for this failure is the change in output in 2.8.x to "no such command:" instead of "no such cmd:". This should be easy to fix :).

Apologies if this is a duplicate.

Cheers,

Tim

summary: Use a better Query data model
description:
The query class should separately model "criteria", "projections", "modifiers" and other query options that go into the wire protocol.

That would simplify the Cursor class -- it would merely hold a Query object and a lazily generate a QueryResult object.  Almost everything else would be delegated methods.

It would also simplify the query methods in the client.  They would take Query objects and return QueryResult objects, rather than consuming a positional list of parameters, some of which are often undefined.

summary: Rationalize cursor batch size control
description:
Currently, there is no public option to control batch size (though limit affect batch size).  We should make batch size public and have actual batch size be the minimum of batch size and limit.

Aggregation cursors also need to support a batch size option.  Python uses a "Command Cursor" class distinct from the regular cursor.  This might be a better approach than the how we fake up an already-running cursor object to return.

summary: Don't use $query when querying against the $cmd collection.
Implement AwaitData
description: Two new query options (AwaitData and Exhaust) and all new response flags (CursorNotFound, QueryFailure, and AwaitCapable) have been added to the Mongo wire protocol (http://www.mongodb.org/display/DOCS/Mongo+Wire+Protocol). The driver should handle the response flags appropriately (eg. CursorNotFound should throw exception rather than silently end the query results). The driver may implement the new query options if desired.

summary: index creation should default to w=1 without an explicit write concern
description: Currently, index creation uses the implicit write concern of the client.  That setting isn't necessarily appropriate for index creation.

summary: Incorrect documentation of cursor snapshot feature
description: Per https://jira.mongodb.org/browse/SERVER-14703, setting snapshot on a cursor doesn't prevent it from omitting documents in the resultset that are updated during the lifetime of the query, they *only* prevent dups. As such, the 'or objects missed' language needs to be changed. The parenthetical '(if an object is new during the query, or deleted during the query, it may or may not be returned, even with snapshot mode)' should become a first-class sentence something like 'If an object is created, deleted, or updated during the lifetime of a query, it may or may not be returned, even with snapshot [set to true (or lang-specific equivalent)].'

summary: ensure_index on arrayref dies if first index type is not numeric
description:
The {{ensure_index}} method has various checks for valid inputs.  One of those checks is this:

{code}
       (ref $keys eq 'ARRAY' &&
         ($#$keys == 0 || $#$keys >= 1 && !($keys->[1] =~ /-?1/))) 
{code}

This dies if the first type is a non-numeric index type.

I think the other, related validation clauses are inconsistent in other ways and they all need to be rationalized.

(Initially reported as [Meerkat #6|https://github.com/dagolden/Meerkat/issues/6])

summary: Support cursor in listCollections and listIndexes commands
Use of scalar refs in combination with numbers seems to create trouble
Perl driver di
description:
I've written a test case which is attached. When I run this program, it fails around 60% of the time with:

  error getting database response: Resource temporarily unavailable

I have googled for "perl mongodb resource temporarily unavailable" with no relevant hits and I've been seeing this bug for quite a while. I've coded around this in my application(s), but is there any way to handle this condition other than Try::Tiny? Is there something I'm doing wrong? 

Thanks in advance for any assistance you can provide.

summary: valid count 0 and error not distinguishable in count result
description:
The current implementation of Cursor::count does not allow to distinguish a 0 result from an error.

In our case, the query coincided with a chunk move. The return document contained "ok" : 0, and a string for "errmsg". run_command returns the errormsg string. This in turn causes count to return 0.

summary: Error Condition documentation / handling -
description: Currently both $err or $errMsg are set separately. It may make sense to add an $errCode with the actual error code to all errors, which would enforce them being cataloged, and then have both $err and $errMsg populated to allow backward compatibility. Assuming users are checking both today, I would be OK just having one of them defined if that is considered a viable option.

summary: Change minHeartbeatFrequencyMS from 10 to 500
GridFS ignores "10014 chunk is empty!" assertion, returns empty string on corrupt files
Invalid use_boolean check
Implement CRUD write API
description: https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/tree/master/source/crud

summary: Add inserted IDs to BulkWriteResult
description: The original Bulk spec didn't require inserted IDs.  The new CRUD spec lists them as optional, but it would be good to add.

summary: Inserting documents as array ref allows duplicate keys
description: Will allow insert/update documents to be provided as an array reference and the code appears to send duplicate key/value pairs to the server, which I think has undefined behavior for duplicates (in practice it appears to take the last value).  We could convert arrays to hashes (losing order) or to IxHash (expensive but preserving order), or we could throw an error if the user tries to use that.

summary: deprecate 'last_error' method on Database class
description: GLE will be pretty much pointless with the shift to write commands.  The Database class should deprecate this method.

summary: Remove 'safe' option in CRUD operations
description: The CRUD spec doesn't allow for a per-operation write concern.  Having new methods not allow it but having old methods with a "safe" option is going to be confusing.  We should get rid of "safe" and rely on the collection-level write concern.

summary: Implement CRUD read API
description: https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/tree/master/source/crud

summary: Consistent ordered document argument validation/coercion
description:
[Github pull 36|https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-perl-driver/pull/36] points out that some functions, like Cursor::sort accept hashes or IxHash but not array references.

Wherever an ordered document is a valid argument, we should have consistent validation/coercion using a type constraint.

summary: Support all query modifiers
description: Not all query modifiers are supported by Cursor class methods.  See http://docs.mongodb.org/manual/reference/operator/query-modifier/

summary: Add a MongoDB::Regex object to avoid issues with compiling stored regexes
Make it easy to clone objects with modified configuration attributes
Deprecate mutable write concern and read preference
description: Having mutable write concern and read preference means they must be re-examined on every request.  We should make them immutable -- set either in MongoClient, or in a Database/Collection/etc. option, or let them be provided per-method.  Users shouldn't have to change them on a MongoClient option, call a method, and then change them back.

summary: Don't modify documents that are missing _id
description: The v0 driver doesn't modify documents to add an _id.  The v1 driver should follow that pattern for consistency.

summary: Update/replace doc validation doesn't respect MongoDB::BSON::char/op_char substitution
description: The Bulk API checks the first key of the update document from a replace or update operation for '$'.  If there was a different {{$MongoDB::BSON::char}} (legacy drivers) or {{op_char} codec option (master branch) in effect, these checks will never find '$'.

summary: Replace legacy author email addresses in metadata and docs
Replace legacy author email addresses in metadata and docs
Add has_modified_count method for Update/Bulk results
description:
Having modified_count equal to undef to signal a pre-2.6 server is confusing in the code. It should be replaced with a boolean attribute like "has_modified_count".

This would make it easier to implement the modified_count accessor so that it dies if not valid, which the new CRUD spec implies should be the behavior.

summary: Rename 'is_safe' to 'is_acknowledged' in WriteConcern
description: "safe" is subjective and the cause of confusion before.

summary: MongoDB::GridFS::File->slurp() dies when reading empty files
Rationalize query_timeout, maxTimeMS and socket_timeout
description:
Prior to maxTimeMS query option, the 'query_timeout' field was used as a read timeout on the socket.  I believe this is equivalent to what other drivers have as socketTimeout.

We should use query_timeout for maxTimeMS on newer servers and fall back to a socket timeout on older servers.

Currently, MongoDB::_Link only supports a single timeout value, so we need to add support for both connection and socket timeouts.

We don't support "socketTimeoutMS" in a connection string, so we could either implement that and have it set query_timeout, or we could add socket_timeout and deprecate query_timeout.

summary: Connection string and constructor attribute precedence is inconsistent
description:
Some attributes in the connection string take precedence over attributes in the constructor and others don't.  This needs to be made consistent and documented.

v0.704.0.0 had username, password and dbname in the connection string take precedence.  v0.704.3.0 added support for other connection options, using the same precedence.  In neither case was precedence documented.

There doesn't appear to be a standard across drivers.  Python has code take precedence over the connection string.

The Perl driver code would be a bit cleaner and more maintainable if the connection string did not take precedence and was merely the fallback if not specified in code.

For comparison, however, the DBI module on CPAN has the connection string take precedence.

summary: Support both connectTimeoutMS and socketTimeoutMS
description: Currently, we only have 'timeout' and that acts as connectTimeoutMS.  We should support both to be consistent with other drivers.  Possibly 'timeout' itself should be deprecated in favor of a more explicit attribute.

summary: Warn on unknown connection string keys
Ignore case for connection string keys
use connect timeout as socket timeout for topology scanning
description:
SDAM mandates that monitoring sockets use the connection timeout for the socket timeout.  Because the Perl driver does both over the same socket and the socket gets configured with parameters at construction, there's no way to do this currently.

The driver doesn't yet even support separate connection and socket timeouts properly (see PERL-398), but even when it does, some design changes will be necessary if monitoring is to use the connection timeout.

summary: Re-implement authMechanismProperties support in connection string
description: The auth implementation predates the finalized spec, which changed the form of authMechanismProperties.

summary: Memory leak when inflating DateTime::Tiny
description: A patch is presented in <https://github.com/chansen/mongo-perl-driver/commit/3a02c0a905f57917387ad2d24858cdb27e2696c0>.

summary: snapshot cursor method needs to take an argument
description:
Right now, it takes no arguments and always turns on snapshotting.  This ought to take an argument -- if defined, use the argument value.

Question -- do we deprecate/warn on the old behavior without an argument?

summary: Deprecate or remove MongoDB::Cursor globals slave_ok and timeout
Add list_collections method
description: list_collections should return a QueryResult for iterating over the collection list

summary: remove always returns the number 1
description:
If I am not mistaken remove is supposed to return a hash with the number of elements it removed, but as far as I can tell the Perl driver always returns the number 1. Even if there was no match and no document was deleted.

http://docs.mongodb.org/manual/reference/method/db.collection.remove/ 

Am I misunderstand something?
If not, would it be possible to return the hash?

summary: Add public method to report server status
description: Users may want to interrogate server status from the Topology.  MongoClient needs a method that scans all servers (possibly only if out of date with respect to heartbeat frequency) and return status in a hash or other data structure.

summary: Saving undef as empty string in db when using Tie::IxHash for document
DBRefs should not require $db field
description:
The MongoDB Manual [describes  dbrefs|http://docs.mongodb.org/manual/reference/database-references/#dbrefs] as having an optional {{$db}} field.  However, the Perl driver made it mandatory for some reason.

Fixing this has unusual back-compatibility considerations:
* existing docs without {{$db}} never got decoded to DBRef objects anyway, so docs with only {{$ref}} and {{$id}} will suddenly wind up decoded as an object when that never happened before
* user code that assumed it would get a document will have problems trying to access it because it's an object, not a hash
* user code that checked if it got a DBRef object and called fetch if it was an object will have problems because without {{$db}}, the fetch code won't work

summary: Allow additional fields in DBRef documents
description: Some drivers allow fields other than '$ref', '$id' and '$db'.  We should ensure those round-trip correctly and may or may not allow creating them.

summary: Inserting Tie::IxHash with _id causes duplicate _id fields
Enforce ordered document constraint on sort fields
description: Sort documents need to be ordered.  Multi-key hashes will randomize, causing surprising behavior.

summary: Compilation errors on OpenBSD and Solaris
description:
OpenBSD is an issue with how pthreads is or isn't linked to the perl binary and how we work around various situations.

Solaris is a single line of libbson that the compiler doesn't like.  The next release of libbson supposedly fixes that so we're waiting for that release.

summary: Max BSON size checks are incorrect
description:
The legacy max BSON size checks are being done against the entire OP_INSERT command, not just the document size(s). There appear to be no size checks for replacement via update.  Bulk API size checks (under development) are likewise approximate for legacy inserts

When BSON encoding is refactored out of wire protocol string construction, it will be possible to check maximum sizes document-by-document and throw an appropriate error if the maximum size is exceeded.

summary: Set topology type to Single for single seed with no replica set name
description:
For consistency with Python and Ruby:

* with a single seed and no replica set name, set topology type to Single
* with a single seed and a replica set name, set topology type to ReplicaSetNoPrimary with that set name
* with multiple seeds, set topology type to Unknown and let the topology manager discover the type

This may mean the "connect_type" parameter can go away.

summary: v1 alpha slower than v0 stable
description:
New driver is even slower than old. 

Id did some benchmarks -> http://pastebin.com/7piGtp2Z
Old driver wasn't so bad, but is had no async interface, so i created fork MongoDB::Async and optimized it little bit.

But new version is TOTALLY UNUSABLE! 700 one element queries in second? WHAT? More than 1ms to send several kb over local loopback?
I can write an app - simple http api doing 1-2 queries and returning result. And it'll process 1-3k queries in second easily in one process, using MongoDB::Async or even old MongoDB driver. But this driver will consume more resources, than app itself. 
Do you think it's okay?
MongoDB is hi performance database and i really love it, but with this driver writing in .txt and scanning it every time may be faster, lol.

STOP using oop tolkits and all those getter/setter things. they're SLOW in perl, it isn't c++ or java
Okay, it's hollywar thing, maybe using them is good idea, but in my opinion these are good for weird business logic, not for time critical things like db drivers, driver shouldn't consume 50-90% cpu time in app.
Best solution would be optimizing time critical paths ( get/write queries, socket read/write) as possible.
Please, use direct hash access. Yes, the doesn't have validation and so they less error-proof, but they work LOT FASTER, because perl isn't c++ or java and all type checking happens in runtime, it's really slow.

Use less variable( seen  in many places of code), creating variable and using it in once (usually passing to function) is bad, it's not C, there is no compiler to optimize this. Use direct stack access ($_[...])  in time critical places.

When i saw new driver i thought - Cool, now it uses perl for socket communication and i can make asynchronous api for mongo by just replacing some connection related stuff inside. Because i'm to lazy and have no time to maintain MongoDB::Async and port all features from mainline driver to my fork, no one pays me for that, i'm even not a professional perl programmer, this is my hobby. 
But when i did bechmarks - WHAT?! Okay... :( I would need to rewrite all time critical paths of code just to use new mongodb v3 features, because in current state driver is unusable because of it's performance, and i don't think this will change in release

Sorry if this post is too emotional. Just... this performance really makes me sad :(

summary: Optimize insert_many
description: {{insert_many}} calls {{$bulk->insert}} repeatedly, which is slow.  We need a way to map them all into MongoDB::BulkWrite operations in one function call.

description: Clone of PERL-555

summary: Parallel scan method should take a read preference

Bulk operations should return partial result on network failure
description:
Bulk operations that have a network error after the first operation succeeds throw an exception such that the user never sees a partial result from the operations that succeeded.

summary: Optimize fetching all results from a query result
description: Calling "all" on a query result winds up iterating item by item to build up a response.  However, in the v1.x branch, it should be possible to pull off all result documents in one go before getting the next batch.  That should reduce function call overhead dramatically.

summary: cursor created by function aggregate is slower than cursor created by function find
description:
When I compare the actual time it takes to retrieve an object using cursor->next with the Perl driver the result clearly shows that cursor created by the find function is twice as fast as a cursor created by the aggregate function.

I've verified that find and aggregate return exactly the same data so the issue is not the amount of data they return.
The query is verify simple:

find({ env => 'TEST'})->sort({hostname => 1});
aggregate( [ { '$match' => { env => 'TEST' } }, { '$sort' => { hostname => 1 } } ] );

The total number of records in the collection is 6635 and the query return 5095 of them.

Why is the cursor created by aggregation twice as slow as the one created by find?

summary: Document loss of precision when encoding long doubles
description: Perl can be compiled with long double support, but BSON encoding works by casting Perl's internal value to double.  -This can overflow.  We should detect this situation and throw an error.-

summary: Check findAndModify results for write concerns
description:
Per Craig Wilson:

{quote}
When the writeConcern on findAndModify fails, the resulting document
still comes back with ok: 1. This is the same as the write commands.
Hence, to check for write concern and raise the appropriate error to
your users, you will need to check the resulting document for a
writeConcernError field. If it is present, then writeConcern failed.
{quote}

summary: CI: GridFS tests fail on Windows
Tests fail on x86 without use64bitint perl
description:
Tests fails like this:

t/legacy/08-string.t ..... ok
    # No tests run!
#   Failed test 'No tests run for subtest "datetime"'
#   at t/legacy/10-bson.t line 434.
During encode_one, Don't know how to encode Math::BigFloat '151109408000' as an Int64. at /builddir/build/BUILD/BSON-v1.2.2/blib/lib/BSON.pm line 299.
 at t/legacy/10-bson.t line 385.
# Looks like your test exited with 255 just after 11.
t/legacy/10-bson.t ....... 
Dubious, test returned 255 (wstat 65280, 0xff00)
Failed 7/17 subtests 

This is because BSON::Time::BUILDARGS() creates Math::BigFloat object but, BSON::PP::_pack_int64() support only native integers, Math::BigInt, and Math::Int64 objects.

The _pack_int64() is probably called from BSON::PP::_encode_bson() when BSON::Time is to be serialized.

summary: Resolve localhost via IPv4
description:
If mongodb is bound only to IPv4 and not IPv6, connections to IPv6 will fail.  Some computers return the IPv6 address first in the list of addresses.  This has been seen most often with Windows machines while connecting to a MongoDB on localhost.

As a workaround, we could connect directly to 127.0.0.1 instead of localhost.  Python does something similar, see [this code|https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-python-driver/commit/e3f3bf23ea03a596478c6116db3b255732b9f14e].

For the upstream IO::Socket::IP bug, see [rt.cpan.org#105679|https://rt.cpan.org/Public/Bug/Display.html?id=105679].

summary: Arm and Power8 requires -lpthreads on non-threaded perls
description: CI builds reveal that Arm64 and Power8 builds need {{-lpthreads}} when building the driver or the shared library fails to load due to missing symbols.

summary: Fix MongoDB::Cursor hint() method's documentation
description: Tracking ticket for [Github PR #139|https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-perl-driver/pull/139].

summary: Document that GridFS filename+uploadDate must be unique
description:
In perldoc for MongoDB::GridFSBucket you say

filename – the name of this stored file; this does not need to be unique

but it does. Entries are keyed by iso datetime and filename (as far as I see it) so if you add two files within the same second you get

MongoDB::GridFSError: Error inserting file document: MongoDB::DuplicateKeyError: E11000 duplicate key error collection: xxx.yyy.files index: filename_1_uploadDate_1 dup key: { : "fn", : new Date(1498494799000) } at (eval 1029) line 31

I don't consider this as major as having unique filenames is not such a problem, though I encountered that hoping blindly that I could overwrite files saving some time on checking first.

description: Tracking ticket for [PR #141|https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-perl-driver/pull/141]

Cache SCRAM-SHA-1 ClientKey
Unacknowledged insert_many throws an error

description: Spec says on tailable await cursors, should not send maxTimeMS/maxAwaitTimeMS unless set and driver sends it as "0".

summary: Orchestrated devel tests are broken
description: Devel tests are broken due to changes in server log output, plus issue deprecation warnings.  They should be overhauled to get passing again without warnings.

summary: Windows tests with SASL fail with free to wrong pool in global destruction
description:
This looks like a threads bug in one of the XS SASL libraries we use.  It only occurs in t/threads/cursor.t and only on 3.0+ servers (meaning we're using SASL for SCRAM-SHA-1).

Workaround is to run those tests on Windows if auth is enabled.

Backport 'PERL-769 Repeated find_one queries result in MongoDB::ProtocolError sometimes' to 1.8.1
description: Backport [PERL-769 Repeated find_one queries result in MongoDB::ProtocolError sometimes|PERL-769] to 1.8.1

summary: Backport PERL-831: Windows tests with SASL fail with free to wrong pool in global destruction
description:
This looks like a threads bug in one of the XS SASL libraries we use.  It only occurs in t/threads/cursor.t and only on 3.0+ servers (meaning we're using SASL for SCRAM-SHA-1).

Workaround is to run those tests on Windows if auth is enabled.

summary: Add support for a "comment" parameter to the aggregate command
Timeout configs not supported for older IO::Socket::IP
Kerberos authentication not working in 1.8.0 version
Index drops on non-existent collection shouldn't throw errors
description: We filter out non-existent namespace errors from listing indexes and should do the same for dropping indexes.  The rationale is that index drops are idempotent and drops on a non-existent collection are "true by definition" -- afterwards indexes (still) don't exist.

summary: Username and password must be URI-escaped
BSON::Timestamp doesn't validate uint32_t limits on seconds and increment
description: BSON::Timestamp should validate the range of its inputs.

summary: Unused call_pv_va() function
Improve auth error messages
description: Auth errors wind up reporting nested error classes.  We should nest only the messages, not the classes.

summary: Wide character in username causes errors encoding auth document
BSON::Timestamp should overload comparison
description: Sessions make heavy use of timestamp comparison. To make life easy we should overload comparison.

summary: Repeated credential validation skips validation after failure
description: Due to a subtlety in hash iteration with the {{each}} keyword, after throwing an exception during iteration of the hash of credential validators, subsequent validation continues iteration rather than starting over.

summary: prefer_numeric interprets dual vars as strings, not numbers
description: Dual vars (PVIV and PVNV) are being interpreted as strings due to the presence of the POK flag.  The MongoDB driver historically used IOK/NOK first before acting on the prefer_numeric config option.  There was no coverage of this expectation so we need tests and a fix.

summary: Drop dependency on DateTime module
description:
The DateTime module is heavy and slow and we want to avoid it being the default for inflated BSON datetime fields in v2.0.0.

If we can drop it entirely from our deep dependency chain, then we can cut out several dependencies, some of which have been seen to be flaky from time to time and give users trouble installing.

Users who want DateTime inflation will be able to do so via the BSON::Date methods to do inflation on demand if the module is available.

summary: SDAM does not gossip $clusterTime
Command Monitoring spec tests should use the same format for bulk writes
description:
There are three changes, the first two are breaking. 

The two breaking changes are in three command-monitoring tests: "bulkWrite.yml", "unacknowledgedBulkWrite.yml" and "insertMany.yml":

https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/commit/bebf9b688f64afdec46a860f102bad0841b04c02

The format of the "requests" array and "ordered" option changes from this:
{noformat}
    operation:
      name: "bulkWrite"
      arguments:
        requests:
          - insertOne: 
              document: { _id: 4, x: 44 }
        ordered: false
{noformat}
To this:
{noformat}
    operation:
      name: "bulkWrite"
      arguments:
        requests:
          - name: "insertOne"
            arguments:
              document: { _id: 4, x: 44 }
        options:
          ordered: false
{noformat}
This brings Command Monitoring tests in line with the Retryable Writes and forthcoming Transactions tests.

The non-breaking change is in the Retryable Writes tests:

https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/commit/c0ce96daac5661f2603bfa52c9b3f683c176a292

Before, the tests said in prose that all MongoClients should be created with retryWrites=true. Now, the tests include the following YAML:
{noformat}
        clientOptions:
            retryWrites: true
{noformat}
The clientOptions are the same for all Retryable Writes tests. Interpreting this field now will prepare you for the Transactions tests, which make real use of the field.

summary: Update ChangeStream spec to detail usage of startAtOperationTime
description:
If drivers have already implemented DRIVERS-464, they need to update their implementation of how they initially store {{startAtOperationTime}}. Instead of setting a default {{startAtOperationTime}} when the ChangeStream is created, drivers must omit a default {{startAtOperationTime}}. When resuming, drivers must use the {{operationTime}} of the initial aggregate command as the default {{startAtOperationTime}} value.

See https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/commit/745debcf4624ec820eb9b138cd8f28fa7b1e5782 for the exact implementation details.

summary: Improve BSON error messages
description:
See DRIVERS-281 but necessary changes for Perl driver:

* Report bad field types in hex notation.
* Add a note to the error that users should check for a newer driver.
* If possible ("SHOULD") report the field name in the error message. 
* -Backport this change to the 0.x driver.-

Reporting the field name will be tricky. This probably means throwing the exception higher up the stack where the field name is available.  This is tricky for arrays as knowing that element 3 of some unknown array had a bad type isn't very useful.  Possibly we could cascade an error string upwards with field names being prepended, but that seems fragile.

summary: Error at connecting when using mongodb+srv style URI
description:
When using something like this.

$mongodb = MongoDB::MongoClient->new( 'host' => mongodb+srv://....../

                                                                           .... )

You will receive the following or similar error:

Value "1567804623.2687" did not pass type constraint "Int" (in $self->\{"expires"}) at /usr/local/share/perl5/MongoDB/_URI.pm line 599

The reason is obvious:

In the begining of _URI.pm file built in time funtion is over defined by Time::Hires time function.

Perl built in time function returns integers but Time:HiRes module time function return fractions as seconds.

Possible FIX: 

remove this line from _URI.pm

use Time::HiRes qw(time);

summary: Error at connecting when using mongodb+srv style URI and boolean parameters
description:
$mongodb = MongoDB::MongoClient->new( 'host' => mongodb+srv://....../?ssl=true

                                                                           .... )

You will receive the following or similar error:

expected boolean string 'true' or 'false' for key 'ssl' but instead received '0'. Ignoring 'ssl'.

Instead of ssl parameter, the error will occur with any boolean parameter.

The reason is that in _URI.pm when connection URI contains mongodb+srv style protocol, sub _parse_srv_uri  pre-parses the URI and call __str_to_bool for each boolean parameters, It transforms each true/false value strings to 1/0 values correspondingly. So when after this pre-parsing phase, the normal  parsing phase occurs in sub _initialize_from_uri and __str_to_bool is called again for each boolean parameter if fails because all boolean parameters are already trasformed to 0/1 but __str_to_bool only accepts true/false as boolean value.

Possible quick FIX: 

alter sub __str_to_bool to accept values true/false and 1/0 for boolean parameters:

or alter sub _parse_srv_uri not to transform boolean parameter strings but leave them as-is.

bson/bson-context.c:51:1: error: conflicting types for ‘gettid’
51 | gettid (void)
| ^~~~~~
In file included from /usr/include/unistd.h:1170,
from bson/bson-compat.h:65,
from bson/bson-context.c:17:
/usr/include/bits/unistd_ext.h:34:16: note: previous declaration of ‘gettid’ was here
34 | extern __pid_t gettid (void) __THROW;
| ^~~~~~
